response:
# TCP vs UDP: Full Comparison
TCP and UDP are the two core transport layer protocols in the TCP/IP internet suite, designed for opposing priorities: TCP prioritizes guaranteed, error-free data delivery, while UDP prioritizes low-latency, lightweight communication. Below is a detailed side-by-side comparison:

| Comparison Metric                | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)                                                                 | UDP (User Datagram Protocol)                                                                 |
|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Core Definition                   | Connection-oriented, reliable ordered delivery protocol engineered for perfect data integrity              | Connectionless, lightweight protocol for best-effort, low-latency data delivery             |
| Connection Model                  | Uses a 3-way SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK handshake to establish a dedicated connection; runs a 4-way teardown handshake after transmission completes | No connection setup or teardown: Sends independent datagrams directly to the target without pre-authorization |
| Reliability & Flow Control        | 100% reliable: Auto-retransmits lost/corrupted packets, acknowledges all received data, filters duplicates, and uses flow control to prevent fast senders from overwhelming slow receivers | No built-in reliability guarantees: Drops packets during network congestion, no retransmissions, no delivery acknowledgments. Custom reliability logic can be added on top (e.g., QUIC for HTTP/3) |
| Packet Ordering                   | Automatically rearranges out-of-order packets into the exact sequential order required by the receiver    | No ordering enforcement: Packets may arrive out of sequence; the receiving application must handle reordering if needed |
| Header Size                       | 20–60 bytes (minimum 20 bytes, up to 60 total bytes with optional fields like window scaling or timestamps) | Fixed 8-byte header: Only contains source port, destination port, packet length, and checksum |
| Congestion Control                | Built-in congestion control: Slows transmission rate during network congestion to avoid network collapse | No native congestion control: Sends packets at full application speed, with no logic to throttle traffic during peaks |
| Data Transmission Model           | Stream-oriented: Treats data as a continuous byte stream, with no preserved original message boundaries (separate writes may be combined or split) | Datagram-oriented: Each packet is an independent, self-contained message that preserves original message framing |
